items.title: Mother whose two children died suddenly calls for genetic testing

children.content: A Perth woman who lost two children to an extremely rare medical condition calls for mandatory genetic testing for children whose cause of death is uncertain.

gallery.altText: A mother with blonde hair and a white dress stands in front of the ocean holding a young baby girl in a white outfit.
A young baby boy dressed in a red and green Christmas elf outfit sits on a couch smiling and laughing.
A smiling baby girl sits on the beach with a bucket wearing a white and green outfit and hat.

items.title: Search for swimmer resumes at Bunbury, police call for information

children.content: Water police search for a swimmer who was reported to be in distress at Koombana Bay in Bunbury on Wednesday.

image.altText: A white ute with writing on it and surfboards on top of tray

items.title: Refinery on WA's south coast says it could replace region's diesel with biofuel

children.content: In the 1990s, Esperance was seen as a key to a major biofuels industry for Western Australia. Thirty years later, the industry has seen little change. But some say the area has untapped potential as a major fuel supplier.

items.title: WA leading the charge as batteries disrupt global energy

children.content: As the use of big batteries booms across the world, Western Australia in particular is proving that grid-scale installations are no longer a complementary technology but can play a central role in power grids.

gallery.altText: rows of battery containers at sunset

thumbnail.altText: rows of battery containers at sunset
A man in high-vis wearing a hard hat stands before rows of white shipping-container-sized batteries

items.title: Emergency hay deliveries resume for WA stations after 'generous' donations

children.content: Emergency hay deliveries to drought-stricken areas resume after thousands of dollars in donations from school students and charity groups.

image.altText: a machine is loading hay bales

items.title: Social media group calls for change while exposing inequality in sport

children.content: Not All Clubs calls out inequality in sporting organisations across the country. This weekend, the group is calling on teams to wear red armbands in memory of the women whose lives have been claimed by gender-based violence this year.

image.altText: Two AFL players stand in a huddle. Both players wear two red bands on their left arm.

items.title: Specific needs 'soar' across school system over past decade, principal says

children.content: WA teachers will be taught how to better support children with higher needs amid rising rates of disability diagnoses, after a report found public schools needed to do more.

gallery.altText: Students are having a math class.
Two girls are having a class in a classroom.
A principal smiles to his students in a classroom.

thumbnail.altText: Students are having a math class.
Two girls are having a class in a classroom.
A principal smiles to his students in a classroom.

items.title: Rodent faeces, animal blood, mould spark $60k penalty for country bakery

children.content: Inspectors found animal blood dripping onto bread and rodent faeces on food at the Albany French Hot Bread Shop, triggering prosecution and $60,000 in penalties for the owners.

gallery.altText: A wide of a shop front

thumbnail.altText: A wide of a shop front
Close of three awards behind a window

items.title: ASX falls, government targets tech giants with tough scam laws

children.content: The Australian share market is trading lower while the federal government proposes tougher scam laws targeting tech giants. Follow the day's events and insights from our business reporters on the ABC News live markets blog.

items.title: Why is Australia experiencing summer-like storms days out from winter?

children.content: A series of low-pressure systems are drenching Australia this week, quickly turning this May into one of the wettest on record for parts of the country's east.

image.altText: Dark grey clouds and rain above a carpark with palm trees in it.

items.title: Student says WA school closed by toxic baits had been overrun by mice

children.content: A year 12 student at the WA College of Agriculture Morawa says there were fears of "cooking" mice in the heating system before the mistaken deployment of toxic baits shut the school down.

gallery.altText: A sign for The Western Australian College of Agriculture Morawa campus in wood and gold metal
A close up of dead mice laying on the brown dirt
A mouse on a stove; a mouse up a wall

thumbnail.altText: A sign for The Western Australian College of Agriculture Morawa campus in wood and gold metal
A close up of dead mice laying on the brown dirt
A mouse on a stove; a mouse up a wall

items.title: Man joins brother and late mother's reconciliation journey after discovering he is Indigenous

children.content: Iain Seymour never knew he was Aboriginal until finding out he was adopted three years ago and then meeting his brother Allen Edwards, the son of a woman who played a key part in setting up a poignant memorial to the long-term effects of the Stolen Generations.
Warning: This story contains images of Indigenous people who have died.

image.altText: Two men with arms on each other's shoulders looking at a fire

items.title: Four Corners reporter Mahmood Fazal sacked by ABC

children.content: Mahmood Fazal has been sacked by the ABC following an internal investigation into an appearance on a podcast hosted by a former underworld figure, managing director Hugh Marks says.

image.altText: A man with a full black beard.

items.title: 'We need to talk about sex': Young people call for sex ed to grow up

children.content: A long-running survey of Australian teens has identified falling condom use and unwanted sex as major concerns for young people starting relationships.

image.altText: The back of students' heads as they lean across a diagram of sexual organs on the desk.

items.title: Grocery shopping is 'habitual' but small changes can help save money

children.content: Small changes to the way we approach our meal planning can make a big difference to the overall cost.

image.altText: A person's hand holding a receipt near a trolley

items.title: Council buys land for $1, but what's the catch?

children.content: A Midwest city council buys Crown land for $1 to develop more than 100 dwellings to house key workers, but WA's shadow local government minister says it is an example of more responsibility being placed on councils.

gallery.altText: A drone image of a block of land with bushland on surrounded by buildings, road and the beach.
Image of a vacant block of land covered in bushland and weeds.
Image of mayor Jerry Clune wearing glasses, a jumper with a collard shirt. He has short hair and a beard. Stands infront of tree

items.title: Why this photo has been terrifying the internet for years

children.content: Forget about slashers and body horror — the next evolution of fright stems from an fluorescent-lit room.

items.title: Young saviour and an answered prayer deliver Blues incredible Origin win

children.content: When the Blues needed heroes to get on top of the Maroons in State of Origin I, they turned to a debutante and the oldest player on the field.

image.altText: Ethan Strange celebrates a Blues try in State of Origin I.

items.title: Hugh Marks denies 'threat' to force ABC news director out of role

children.content: ABC managing director Hugh Marks says he did not "make threats" to terminate departing news boss Justin Stevens, as senators question his sudden departure.

image.altText: A man looking pensive, walking towards the camera wearing a suit and carrying a clipboard.

items.title: BP removes chair over 'serious concerns' about conduct

children.content: The BBC reported Albert Manifold's departure followed concerns about "bullying" and "overbearing" behaviour, which he later described as a "false narrative".

image.altText: A man smiling in a suit.

items.title: The BHP files reveal a big problem facing the mining giant

children.content: For a company that prides itself on digging out the metals for the world's clean energy future, saying nothing about its own clean energy future is not an option.

articles.title: WA iron ore processing project dumped by BHP for not being profitable enough

children.content: Internal BHP documents reveal how the mining giant planned to set up an iron ore processing facility in the Pilbara to cut carbon emissions before backing away from it entirely.

image.altText: A photo of an iron ore mine is ripped down the middle and replaced by a photo of a BHP building.

articles.title: Leaked documents about WA mines reveal how far BHP really is from net zero goals

children.content: The mining giant's delay in the rollout of renewable energy and a fleet of electric trucks and trains in the Pilbara has raised doubts inside the company about it meeting its ambitious climate goal.

image.altText: A large yellow mining truck drives along a raise road. It has a load in its tray.

gallery.altText: Mining infrastructure and machinery in pits of red dirt.
Three newspapers with the same ad which says 'BHP has reduced emissions by 36 per cent' and has graphs.

thumbnail.altText: Mining infrastructure and machinery in pits of red dirt.
Three newspapers with the same ad which says 'BHP has reduced emissions by 36 per cent' and has graphs.
